title: Yes and Amen in Christ
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Yes and Amen in Christ_ 2 Corinthians 1_20 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt
outline: Introduction: 2 Corinthians 1:20 — all the promises of God
For all the promises of God in Him are Yes
And in Him Amen, to the glory of God through us
The faithfulness of God to all His covenant promises
Christ as the fulfillment and confirmation of every promise
The response of the church: Amen to God's glory
Conclusion: Living in the Yes and Amen of Christ
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches from 2 Corinthians 1:20 on the glorious declaration that all the promises of God find their Yes and Amen in Christ. He expounds the faithfulness of God to every covenant promise, showing that Christ is the ultimate fulfillment, and the church's proper response is the Amen of faith to God's glory.
transcript:
God's covenant faithfulness is fully and finally fulfilled in Jesus Christ.

I want to warn you about a certain approach to Scripture — the redemptive-historical method of interpretation that says we should seek Christ in every passage. That's not quite how we should always approach the Scriptures. And yet the Scriptures themselves tell us that the fulfillment of all things is in Christ. Everything is moving toward Christ in the Scriptures. And we see that in our passage: every promise God has ever made finds its certainty, fulfillment, and assurance in Christ alone.

## The Problem: A World of Broken Promises

We live in a world saturated with broken promises — political promises, business promises, personal promises. Even well-intentioned people fail to deliver on their promises sometimes. Over time we learn that promises alone don't guarantee outcomes. Just because somebody said they're going to do something doesn't mean they will.

Because that's the world we live in, we might bring that same skepticism into our spiritual life — where we don't think God is going to do what he said he's going to do, that he's not going to be faithful to the promises he gave us. We may even reach a point where we believe that God *can* keep his promises but struggle to believe that he *will*, because we've experienced what feels like unfaithfulness, or whatever it was — we think God will not keep his promises.

The Corinthian church was struggling with this very issue. They questioned Paul's reliability, and by extension they questioned the gospel's reliability — the very gospel Paul had preached to them. Paul had changed his travel plans, and some accused him of being untrustworthy, of saying yes and no at the same time. Paul responds by lifting their eyes far above himself in his answers. He doesn't say, "Look at me, how good I am." He lifts their eyes above himself and points them to Christ, who is the one who is faithful. He points them to something unshakable: God's promises fulfilled in Christ.

## The Context: God Is Not Fickle

Our goal this afternoon is to look at verse 20 of chapter 1. Before we get there we need to understand the context, which is this: God is not fickle. He does not change. He can be trusted.

Here Paul is defending his integrity and the integrity of his ministry — but he does so theologically, not defensively. You see that in verses 18 and 19. This is Paul's logic: God is faithful. I am a minister of that God. Therefore that's my pattern. I follow what God says and what God does. I'm not faithful because I'm a great person. I'm faithful because my God is faithful. Christ is faithful. Therefore I'm also faithful.

The point Paul is making is that his ministry reflects God's character. The gospel doesn't vacillate because God doesn't vacillate. The gospel doesn't change because God doesn't change. This is a crucial Reformed conviction: the faithfulness of the gospel rests upon divine immutability, not on human consistency. The gospel is the same because Jesus is the same yesterday, today, and forever. God doesn't change his mind. God does not revise his plan. God does not overpromise and underdeliver.

## The Scope: *All* the Promises of God

Look at verse 20:

> *"For all the promises of God in Him are Yes, and in Him Amen, to the glory of God through us."*

That is the scope of God's faithfulness: *all* his promises. Paul doesn't say some of his promises. Paul doesn't say most of his promises. Paul doesn't say the easy ones. Paul says all the promises of God are yes and amen in Jesus Christ.

From Genesis to Malachi and beyond — every covenant promise, every prophecy, every type, every shadow finds its fulfillment in Christ.

- The promise to Adam that the seed of the woman would crush the serpent's head (Genesis 3:15) — fulfilled in Christ's victory over sin and Satan.
- The promise to Abraham, *"In you all the nations of the earth shall be blessed"* — fulfilled in Christ, the true seed of Abraham, as Paul tells us in Galatians 3.
- The promise to Moses that a prophet greater than Moses would come — fulfilled in Christ, the final mediator.
- The promise to David, the eternal king who would reign forever — fulfilled in Christ, the ultimate Son of David, enthroned at God's right hand.
- The promise of the new covenant — the new heart, forgiveness of sins, God dwelling with his people — fulfilled in Christ's shed blood. *"This is my blood, the blood of the new covenant, shed for you."*

You can see in the Bible this unified story culminating in Christ, who is the fulfillment of all the promises of God.

## Christ Is the Promise Itself

This is of major importance. Christ is not merely a footnote in history. Christ is the fulfillment of all the promises of God. Paul does not say that the promises are *about* Christ. He says they are *yes in Him*. That means Christ is not merely the messenger of the promises. He is the substance of them. Christ *is* the promise.

Do you understand that? God's promise is not a better life for you. God's promise is not even heaven, at least ultimately. God's promise is not an easy marriage or success in life. Even a clear conscience is not the ultimate promise. The ultimate promise is Christ himself.

As someone once said (though the saying goes back through history): he who has Christ and all the things of the world has nothing more than he who has Christ alone. Because Christ is the ultimate promise.

- Christ is the ultimate Israel.
- Christ is the ultimate temple.
- Christ is the final sacrifice.
- Christ is the perfect high priest.

Every promise finds its certainty in him, its substance in him, its security in him.

And outside of Christ, God's promises do not comfort us. If you would be in heaven and Christ were not there, you would not be a believer — because heaven would be hell if Christ weren't present. Outside of Christ, God's promises don't comfort; rather, they condemn, because the promises of blessing are covenant promises, and in covenant, blessings accompany covenant obedience. And the only one whose obedience is sufficient before God is the obedience of Christ, the perfect Lamb of God.

So God's promises are not fulfilled by our sincerity. They are not fulfilled by our faithfulness. They are not fulfilled by our effort.

In Brazil, as a strong Roman Catholic country, there is the practice of the *promessa* — a vow made to God: "God, if you heal my mother of this disease, I'll climb the stairs of that church on my knees," or "I won't drink alcohol for a year." As if our efforts would bind God to do certain things. But God's promises are not fulfilled by our efforts. They are fulfilled by Christ's obedience, Christ's righteousness, Christ's blood.

And when we understand that, this guards us from an empty moralism and drives us to Christ alone. It doesn't matter what list you have. It doesn't matter how carefully you keep those things. It is not your obedience — it is Christ's life, death, and resurrection that secures all God's blessings and promises for you.

## The Amen: Our Response Through Christ

Verse 20 continues:

> *"...and in Him Amen, to the glory of God through us."*

Our "Amen" — our response of faith — is *through* him. And this amen is not wishful thinking, not optimism. This is the amen of knowing that God is faithful to what he said in his covenantal promises. To say "amen" here is to say: This is true. This is certain. I will stake my life on this.

Notice that we do not say amen *to ourselves* — we say amen *through Christ*. Faith itself is mediated by Christ. This is the ground of our salvation. Christ is the object of our faith. Christ is the mediator of our response. That's why we come with our amen through him.

Even our believing is upheld by the grace of God. The fact that you have not denied Christ and can say amen through him today is because the grace of God is sustaining you.

Do you know who Atlas is? Not the atlas where you look up maps — Atlas was one of the figures of Greek mythology. You've seen the image: the man carrying the world on his back. Well, you are safe today. You remain safe today — not because Atlas is carrying the world, but because Jesus continues to sustain you by his grace. And the beautiful thing is that it doesn't take any great effort on his part, because as Hebrews 1 says, *"He upholds all things by the word of his power."* According to some estimates, there are three billion Christians in the world today. Every one of us stays saved because Christ just speaks — and that sustains us.

## The Goal: The Glory of God

> *"...to the glory of God through us."*

The ultimate purpose of all of this is not our salvation. It's not our well-being. It's the glory of God. Everything is to the glory of God. The final goal is not our comfort — though we do receive comfort through the promises of God. The final goal is the glory of God. God keeps his promises not to make much of us, but to magnify his Son. To make the Son look as big as he is.

And when God fulfills his promises in Christ, grace is exalted, human boasting is silenced, and worship is increased and purified at the same time. The certainty of these promises fuels the doxology. Truth like this fuels living a life that displays the glory of God.

## Application: Living in the Yes and Amen

Let me try to give you three ways in which this can be lived out.

**For the doubting believer:** If you are in Christ, God has never broken and will never break a promise to you. Your feelings may waver. Your circumstances may confuse you. But Christ does not change. Take your doubts to him. Too often, when we have doubts, instead of taking them to the Bible, to the Spirit, and to the church, we take them to people who despise God and despise the Word. Take your doubts to Christ — he is not going to abandon you.

**For the suffering Christian:** The promises of God are not suspended by suffering. They are not there only for the times when suffering is absent. They are for suffering times as well — they are confirmed *through* suffering. Paul writes these words in the context of affliction. Christ is still yes. Christ is still amen in your suffering.

**For the self-righteous:** Outside of Christ, promises don't save — they actually expose your need of Christ. If you're trusting anything other than Christ for God to see you as his own, you have no hope, you have no promise, and your end is destruction. God doesn't want your righteousness — he already has the righteousness of Christ. He wants you to come to him as a broken sinner. And Christ will take care of that.

Every promise God has made has already received its answer in Christ. And that answer is not "maybe." It's not "eventually." That answer is yes. And so by faith, through Christ, we say amen — to the glory of God alone.
